name: Hammer
description: A heavy steel hammer with a wooden handle darkened by years of use. The head is pocked with tiny craters from striking things harder than steel.
secret: The hammer head is made from a dimensional alloy that transfers force differently depending on what it strikes — soft on soft materials, hard on hard ones. Skilled crafters use it to shape materials that would shatter under a normal hammer.

properties.takeRefusal: The tools stay at the forge. House rules.

name: Tongs
description: Long-handled iron tongs for gripping hot materials. The tips are scarred and slightly misaligned from heavy use.
secret: The tongs' misalignment is intentional — the tips create a slight twist when gripping, which is the correct way to handle coppervine. Grip it straight and it fights you; grip it with a twist and it relaxes.

properties.takeRefusal: The tools stay at the forge. House rules.

name: Mortar and Pestle
description: A heavy stone mortar with a matching pestle, both worn smooth in the bowl. Traces of different colored powders stain the rim in layers.
secret: The layered powder residue in the mortar acts as a catalyst. Grinding a new material here mixes in trace amounts of everything previously ground, which occasionally produces unexpected results — a happy accident the regulars rely on.

properties.takeRefusal: The tools stay at the forge. House rules.

name: Shimmerite Dust
description: A small pouch of fine, iridescent powder. It catches the light differently depending on the angle — silver, then violet, then a color you don't have a word for. Tiv keeps it in a sealed pouch because it tends to drift.
secret: Shimmerite is condensed light from a high-luminosity dimension. It bonds permanently with any material that can absorb it — clay, fiber, certain resins. Once bonded, the host material becomes light-responsive: it glows, senses nearby light sources, or stores light for later release. It repels void glass on its own but the two can be bridged through an intermediary like coppervine.

name: Coppervine Wire
description: A coil of thin wire with a greenish-copper sheen. It's not quite metal — it flexes like a living tendril and faintly resists being straightened, as if it remembers the shape of whatever vine it was drawn from.
secret: Coppervine is still alive — dormant, but alive. It conducts vibration as well as electricity, and wraps tightly around anything that vibrates at the right frequency. Void glass resonates at exactly that frequency, which is why coppervine naturally grips it. Submerged in water, coppervine slowly grows.

name: Sealed Clay
description: A fist-sized lump of dense gray clay wrapped in waxed paper. It's heavier than it looks and slightly warm to the touch. A label in Tiv's handwriting reads: SEAL INTACT — DO NOT WET.
secret: The clay is from a dimension with higher pressure — it's pre-compressed and stays solid at normal pressure but softens when mixed with powder or dust, absorbing and binding with the additive permanently. Water breaks the dimensional seal and the clay expands violently, becoming uselessite foam. The warmth is residual dimensional pressure slowly equalizing.

name: Void Glass Shard
description: A thumb-length sliver of glass so dark it seems to absorb light at its edges. Looking through it makes distant things appear closer, but slightly wrong — reversed, or from an angle that shouldn't be possible.
secret: Void glass is formed in a dimension with almost no electromagnetic radiation. It's hungry for photons, which is why it darkens at the edges. Near unusual materials from other dimensions, it vibrates subtly — the more exotic the material's origin, the stronger the vibration. This makes it a natural detector element. It repels shimmerite directly but resonates with coppervine.

name: Notice Board
description: A weathered wooden board bristling with pinned papers. Requests for rare materials, offers of services, warnings about unreliable vendors, a few sketched maps of market sections. Most are in scripts you can't read, but some have been helpfully illustrated. One recurring notice, pinned in several places, reads: BRING FINISHED GOODS TO THE COLLECTOR — FAIR PRICES PAID.
secret: Some of the notices are written in a script that changes when you're not looking directly at it. One particular notice, pinned under several layers of others, is a map of the market that shows stalls and passages that don't seem to exist yet — or don't exist anymore.

properties.takeRefusal: The board is bolted to the wall.

name: Forge
description: A broad stone hearth fed by bellows that seem to pump themselves. The coals glow a steady orange-white. The heat coming off it is intense but oddly contained — step two paces back and the air is cool again.
secret: The forge burns fuel from a high-energy dimension — the coals don't consume, they cycle. The heat containment is a property of the fuel, not the forge design. Materials heated here briefly take on workable properties: coppervine loses its shape memory and can be bent freely, void glass becomes flexible instead of brittle, sealed clay softens without needing powder. The effects fade as things cool.

name: Workshop Sign
description: A hand-painted wooden sign nailed to a support post: USE THE TOOLS. CLEAN UP AFTER. NO FIGHTING. Below it, in smaller letters: GRIST IS ALWAYS RIGHT. Below that, in different handwriting: (GRIST DID NOT WRITE THAT.)
secret: The sign has been repainted dozens of times. Under the current text, earlier versions had different rules, including one that read: IF IT MOVES ON ITS OWN, PUT IT DOWN AND STEP BACK.

properties.takeRefusal: Leave the sign where it is.

name: Brass Scale
description: A small brass scale on Madame Wick's desk. One pan holds a single white feather. The other pan holds nothing. They balance perfectly.
secret: The scale measures perceived fairness, not weight. Place something in the empty pan and the scale tips or balances depending on whether the offered price matches the value both parties assign to the deal. It can't be fooled — it reads intent, not declared value. Madame Wick uses it to close disputes.

properties.takeRefusal: Madame Wick places one finger on the scale without looking up.

name: Price Board
description: A large slate board mounted on a wooden frame near the court entrance. Prices are chalked in neat columns. None of them are numbers. One reads: "Matched set of memories (two, related)." Another: "Four hours, uninterrupted." A third has been partially erased and rewritten several times.
secret: The prices update themselves. Vendors chalk their terms each morning, but the board occasionally adjusts — a price that was "one secret" yesterday might read "one secret (good)" today. Nobody admits to changing them. The partially erased entry is a price that keeps being set and then reconsidered: someone wants to sell something but can't settle on what to ask.

properties.takeRefusal: The board is bolted to its frame.

name: Slag Chunk
description: A fist-sized lump of glassy residue from the crucibles. It's striated in layers — orange, black, and a blue that shifts when you tilt it. Heavy for its size and faintly warm.
secret: Slag from the Rendering Yards is a mix of dimensional residues fused together. This chunk contains traces of at least three different source dimensions. Ground to powder, it acts as a flux that helps incompatible materials bond — shimmerite and void glass, for instance, which normally repel each other, can be joined using slag powder as an intermediary. Kelp considers slag waste, and will let you take as much as you want.

name: Bag of Roasted Nuts
description: A paper cone of warm roasted nuts. They're golden-brown and smell like cinnamon.
secret: The spice blend enhances perception for about ten minutes after eating. Descriptions of materials the player examines should include subtle hints about hidden properties. The effect is mild — more of a sharpening than a revelation.

name: Iron-Silk Wire
description: A spool of wire so fine it's almost invisible. It catches the light like a strand of spider silk but has the tensile strength of piano wire. Spool cut it to length with a pair of shears that made no sound.
secret: Iron-silk conducts electricity with almost no resistance and is strong enough to suspend weight far beyond what its diameter suggests. Spliced to coppervine, it creates a dual conductor that carries both vibration and electric current — the basis for sophisticated sensors. On its own, coiled tightly around a material, it creates a weak stabilizing field.

name: Pressed Sheet
description: A thin sheet of dark material, flexible enough to bend but stiff enough to hold a shape. It's warm and faintly translucent — hold it up to light and you can see dim shapes through it.
secret: This is a sheet of pressed mixed-dimensional material — the press flattened and fused several scraps together. In sheet form, materials become workable in ways their bulk forms aren't: they can be cut, folded, layered, and wrapped around other objects. A sheet wrapped around a crafted object acts as a casing that stabilizes the internal components.

name: Blank Binding Page
description: A single sheet of heavy, cream-colored paper with a faint texture like woven grass. It feels slightly warm, and the surface has a quality that makes you want to write on it.
secret: Words written on this paper with Quill's ink have a mild binding property — they resist being unsaid or contradicted. A crafting recipe written on this page would be more reliable. A promise written here would be harder to break. The page is blank and waiting.

name: Salt Vendor's Stall
description: A stall of salts in graded jars: cooking salt, curing salt, and salts whose labels are just coordinates. The vendor measures with a horn spoon and an air of ceremony.

scenery.description: The coordinate-labeled jars are the expensive ones. Asking where the coordinates point is, the vendor explains, the first installment.
scenery.rejection: The vendor's horn spoon raps your knuckles with practiced accuracy.

name: Glassblower's Stand
description: A stand of blown glass in shapes that justify themselves slowly. The glassblower works at the back, adding to the inventory by the hour.

scenery.description: One piece is a vessel until you turn it, then it is clearly a lens, then it is a vessel again. It is priced as both.
scenery.rejection: Glass and curiosity are billed together here. A sign says so.

name: Griddle Stand
description: A flat stone griddle heated from beneath by an arrangement under non-disclosure. Something round is always mid-fry.

scenery.description: It is flat, round, golden, and smells like an argument for staying. The vendor flips it without looking, which is the entire advertisement.
scenery.rejection: It is not done. The vendor will tell you when it is done.

name: Hanging Wares
description: Goods strung overhead on a lattice of ropes and disputes: lamps, tools, cages, instruments, and several categories pending classification.

name: Collapsed Stall Frames
description: Frames gone to ruin, soft with rot at the joints. Whatever they displayed went with the sellers.

name: Tin Kettle
description: A dented tin kettle, kept polished where it counts. Warm, very faintly, as if used this morning.
